annotations.text: One syndrome that deserves mention in this case is arrhythmogenic right ventricular dysplasia, which is primarily a right ventricular cardiomyopathy, frequently with little or no left ventricular involvement.
Sustained monomorphic ventricular tachycardia is a frequent finding in this uncommon disorder and can be refractory to treatment.
Although this patient seems to have had disproportionate right ventricular involvement, other clinical findings would not be explained by this diagnosis.
In this case ventricular tachycardia was occurring in the setting of a global cardiomyopathy or myocarditis.
The coxsackie group B viruses are most commonly implicated.
The pattern of lung involvement, although atypical, is consistent with pulmonary sarcoidosis.
Although 20 to 25 percent of patients with sarcoidosis have cardiac involvement at autopsy, only 5 percent of them have clinically overt disease.
the presence of thoracic lymphadenopathy in this young nonsmoker, the possibility of lymphoma should be considered.
I believe that this patient had systemic sarcoidosis involving the heart and lungs.
Generalized cutaneous anergy is typical of sarcoidosis.
The diagnostic procedure was probably a transbronchial biopsy at the time of bronchoscopic examination.
Since previous myocardial biopsies had been nondiagnostic, a repeated myocardial biopsy, even in the light of the progressive left ventricular dysfunction, would not have been my first choice.
